title: Ochrosia minima
intro: Ochrosia minima is a small, flowering evergreen tree native to the tropical regions of India and Southeast Asia, commonly known as the 'Black Olive Tree' due to its dark, olive-colored bark. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties, which may help to reduce fever, alleviate respiratory issues, and protect against certain infections. The bioactive constituents of Ochrosia minima include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, cough, and cold.
taxonomy: Ochrosia minima is classified within the family Apocynaceae, commonly referred to as the dogbane or oleander family. This family of plants includes over 5,000 species worldwide, primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. The genus Ochrosia is a group of flowering plants native to the Pacific Islands and Australia. Ochrosia minima is a species of flowering plant native to the Pacific Islands, including Hawaii and Fiji. The classification of Ochrosia minima is further broken down into its binomial nomenclature, Ochrosia minima, as a specific species within the Apocynaceae family.

properties: Ochrosia minima, also known as the dwarf silver oak, has been traditionally used in Southeast Asian folk medicine for its therapeutic properties. It is believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help to reduce pain and prevent infections. The plant's extracts have also been shown to exhibit antioxidant activity, which can help to protect against cell damage and oxidative stress.
